verdict_reason: BG 2.24 is the technical spec of the imperishable. The risk of trivialization (treating Krishna's list as a tweetable checklist) is foreclosed by the falsifiability field, which explicitly disqualifies the 'API gets nine virtues' read: 'A contract is not eternal because it carries a version number; it is eternal if it actually survives the next migration.' The verse is honoured by being mapped to a real engineering audit-discipline (nine reality-tests, each named by its falsifier). The fintech audit scenario takes the nine-property list seriously enough to identify which of the company's contracts actually meet which properties, with named owners for remediation. A Sanskrit scholar would feel the verse honoured by being given operational specificity rather than reduced to a slogan. PASS.
